How do you know a reaction even happened? Recall the **signs of a chemical change**: a **color change**, a **gas given off** (bubbles), **heat or light** released, or a **solid forming** out of liquids (a precipitate). Spot one of these and new stuff has likely formed. Everyday reactions are all around you: **burning wood** (heat and light), **baking** bread, iron **rusting**, a plant doing **photosynthesis**, a **battery** powering a flashlight, and that **baking-soda-and-vinegar fizz**.

**Recap.** A reaction rearranges atoms into new substances. The starting substances are **reactants**, the new ones are **products**, and we write **reactants → products** where the arrow means 'yields'. Signs a reaction happened: color change, gas, heat or light, or a solid forming.

items.prompt: What does a chemical reaction do?
choices: It rearranges atoms into new substances
It makes atoms vanish completely
It only changes the shape of a substance without making anything new
It creates brand-new atoms out of nothing
items.explanation: A chemical reaction rearranges the atoms you started with into new substances. Atoms are not destroyed (choice 2) or newly created (choice 4); and only changing shape with no new substance is a physical change, not a reaction (choice 3).

items.prompt: In a reaction, what are the REACTANTS?
choices: The new substances you end up with
The starting substances you begin with
The heat given off during the change
The bubbles that escape into the air
items.explanation: Reactants are the starting substances you begin with, written on the left of the arrow. The new substances you end up with are the products (choice 1); heat (choice 3) and bubbles (choice 4) can be signs of the reaction but are not what 'reactants' means.

items.prompt: In a reaction, what are the PRODUCTS?
choices: The starting substances before anything happens
The container the reaction happens in
The new substances formed by the reaction
The leftover atoms that took no part
items.explanation: Products are the new substances the reaction makes, written on the right of the arrow. The starting substances are the reactants (choice 1); the container (choice 2) is not part of the equation; and in a reaction the atoms are rearranged, not left over unused (choice 4).

items.prompt: We write 'reactants → products'. What does the arrow mean?
choices: 'Turns into' or 'yields'
'Is exactly equal in every way to'
'Is much heavier than'
'Is the opposite of'
items.explanation: The arrow means 'turns into' or 'yields' — the reactants make the products. It does not mean simple equality (choice 2), and it says nothing about weight (choice 3) or being an opposite (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the equation 'baking soda + vinegar → fizz', which are the reactants and which is the product?
choices: Fizz is the reactant; baking soda and vinegar are the products
Baking soda and vinegar are the reactants; the fizz is the product
All three are reactants
All three are products
items.explanation: Whatever is on the left of the arrow is the reactant, so baking soda and vinegar are the reactants, and the fizz on the right is the product. Choice 1 reverses the sides, and choices 3 and 4 ignore that the arrow splits reactants from products.

items.prompt: Which of these is a sign that a chemical reaction has happened?
choices: Everything keeps exactly the same color, with no gas, heat, or solid
A gas is given off as bubbles
A liquid is simply poured from one cup to another
An object is moved across the table
items.explanation: Giving off a gas (bubbles) is a classic sign a reaction occurred and new stuff formed. Staying exactly the same signals no reaction (choice 1), and pouring (choice 3) or moving an object (choice 4) are not signs of a chemical change.

items.prompt: Which everyday event is an example of a chemical reaction?
choices: Stacking books into a pile
Stretching a rubber band
Burning wood in a campfire
Sliding a chair across the floor
items.explanation: Burning wood turns it into new substances (ash, smoke, gases) while releasing heat and light, so it is a reaction. Stacking books (choice 1), stretching a band (choice 2), and sliding a chair (choice 4) just move or reshape things without making new substances.

items.prompt: A student says, 'The products go on the left and the reactants go on the right of the arrow.' What is the fix?
choices: The student is right; products are on the left
It is the other way around: reactants are on the LEFT, products are on the RIGHT
Both reactants and products go on the left
The arrow has no left or right side
items.explanation: The reactants (starting substances) are written on the left and the products (new substances) on the right, because the arrow points from what you start with to what you make. The student has the sides swapped (choice 1), and choices 3 and 4 misread how the arrow separates the two sides.

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up this lesson?
choices: A reaction rearranges atoms into new substances; reactants → products, where the arrow means 'yields'
A reaction makes atoms disappear, and reactants equal products in every way
Reactants and products mean the same thing and sit on the same side
A reaction only ever changes the shape of a substance
items.explanation: A reaction rearranges atoms into new substances, written reactants → products with the arrow meaning 'turns into / yields'. Atoms do not disappear (choice 2); reactants and products are different and sit on opposite sides (choice 3); and making only a new shape is a physical change, not a reaction (choice 4).

lessons.title: Conservation of Mass and Balancing Equations
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Take apart a small LEGO model and rebuild the **same bricks** into a new shape. You did not gain or lose a single brick — same bricks, new arrangement. A chemical reaction works the very same way with atoms.

**Build.** The key law is **conservation of mass**: in a reaction, atoms are **not created or destroyed — only rearranged**. You end up with **exactly the atoms you started with**, just recombined into new substances. Because no atoms appear or vanish, the **total mass before equals the total mass after**. That LEGO picture is the whole idea: nothing is created or destroyed, only rebuilt.

This is **why we balance equations**. Since the atoms are conserved, a chemical equation must show the **same number of each kind of atom on BOTH sides** of the arrow. If oxygen atoms do not match left and right, the equation is not yet telling the truth about the atoms.

We balance by putting big numbers, called **coefficients**, in **FRONT** of formulas. A **coefficient multiplies the whole molecule** after it. We must **never** balance by changing a **subscript** (the small low number), because — recall module 6 — changing a subscript changes the substance itself.

Walk one clean example: making water. Start with **H₂ + O₂ → H₂O**. Count: left has 2 O, right has only 1 O — **not balanced**. Fix it with front coefficients: **2 H₂ + O₂ → 2 H₂O**. Now count carefully. Left: 2 of H₂ = **4 H**, and **2 O**. Right: 2 of H₂O = **4 H**, and **2 O**. Both sides show **4 H and 2 O** — balanced!

**Recap.** Conservation of mass: atoms are rearranged, never created or destroyed (the LEGO idea), so each kind of atom must be equal on both sides. Balance using front **coefficients** (which multiply the whole molecule), never by changing **subscripts**.

items.prompt: What does conservation of mass say happens to atoms during a reaction?
choices: New atoms are created to make the products
Some atoms are destroyed and disappear
Atoms are neither created nor destroyed, only rearranged
Atoms turn into pure energy and leave
items.explanation: Conservation of mass means atoms are only rearranged, never created or destroyed, so you end with the same atoms recombined. Atoms are not newly made (choice 1), not destroyed (choice 2), and do not simply vanish as energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: Rebuilding the same LEGO bricks into a new model is a good picture of conservation of mass because:
choices: You end up with extra bricks that were not there before
You lose a few bricks every time you rebuild
It is the same bricks in a new arrangement — nothing created or destroyed
The bricks turn into a completely different material
items.explanation: Just like atoms in a reaction, the LEGO bricks are the same ones rearranged into a new shape — none created or destroyed. You do not gain bricks (choice 1) or lose bricks (choice 2), and the bricks stay the same bricks rather than becoming new material (choice 4).

items.prompt: Because atoms are conserved, a balanced chemical equation must have:
choices: More atoms on the right than on the left
The same number of each kind of atom on both sides
Any number of atoms, as long as it looks tidy
Fewer atoms after the reaction than before
items.explanation: Since no atoms are created or destroyed, a balanced equation has the same number of each kind of atom on both sides. The counts cannot be larger on one side (choice 1), cannot just be whatever looks tidy (choice 3), and cannot shrink (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do we bother balancing chemical equations at all?
choices: To make the equation longer
Because atoms are conserved, so each kind must be equal on both sides
To change the reactants into different elements
Because mass is allowed to appear and disappear in reactions
items.explanation: We balance because atoms are conserved: the same atoms present before must be present after, so each kind must be equal on both sides. Balancing is not about length (choice 1) or changing elements (choice 3), and mass cannot appear or disappear (choice 4).

items.prompt: Is the equation H2 + O2 → H2O balanced? Count the oxygen atoms on each side.
choices: Yes, it is balanced as written
No — there are 2 oxygen on the left but only 1 oxygen on the right
No — there are 5 oxygen on each side
It cannot be decided by counting atoms
items.explanation: On the left, O2 has 2 oxygen atoms; on the right, H2O has only 1 oxygen atom. 2 does not equal 1, so it is not balanced. It is therefore not balanced as written (choice 1), there are nowhere near 5 oxygen (choice 3), and counting atoms is exactly how you decide (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the balanced equation 2 H2 + O2 → 2 H2O, how many hydrogen atoms are on each side?
choices: 4 hydrogen on the left and 4 hydrogen on the right
2 hydrogen on the left and 4 hydrogen on the right
4 hydrogen on the left and 2 hydrogen on the right
2 hydrogen on the left and 2 hydrogen on the right
items.explanation: Left: the coefficient 2 times H2 gives 2 × 2 = 4 hydrogen. Right: the coefficient 2 times H2O gives 2 × 2 = 4 hydrogen. Both sides have 4 H, which is what 'balanced' means. The other choices miscount one side by forgetting to multiply the front number through the molecule.

items.prompt: In 2 H2O, what does the big 2 in front (the coefficient) mean?
choices: There is half a water molecule
There are 2 whole water molecules (so 4 H and 2 O atoms in all)
The water has changed into a different substance
There is exactly 1 water molecule
items.explanation: A coefficient multiplies the whole molecule, so 2 H2O means 2 complete water molecules — that is 4 hydrogen and 2 oxygen atoms in total. It is not half a molecule (choice 1), it does not change what the substance is (choice 3), and it means 2, not 1 (choice 4).

items.prompt: To balance an equation, which number do you change — the coefficient or the subscript?
choices: Change the subscript (the small low number)
Change the coefficient (the big number in front)
Change either one; it makes no difference
You never change any number
items.explanation: You balance by changing the coefficient, the big number in front, which multiplies the whole molecule. You must not change the subscript, because that changes the substance itself (choice 1). It does matter which you change (choice 3), and balancing does require adjusting coefficients (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student tries to balance oxygen by changing H2O into H2O2. Why is that wrong?
choices: It is fine; changing the subscript is the normal way to balance
Changing the subscript changes the substance — H2O2 is no longer water
It is wrong only because the numbers get bigger
H2O2 is just another way to write water
items.explanation: Changing a subscript changes what the substance is: H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide) is not water at all. So you balance with front coefficients instead, never by editing subscripts. Choice 1 endorses the mistake, choice 3 gives the wrong reason, and choice 4 is false since H2O2 is a different compound.

items.prompt: A friend weighs the reactants, runs a reaction in a sealed jar, and weighs the products. What should the total mass be?
choices: Heavier than before, because new substances were made
Lighter than before, because some atoms were destroyed
The same as before, because atoms are only rearranged
Zero, because the reactants are used up
items.explanation: By conservation of mass, the atoms are only rearranged, so the total mass stays the same in a sealed jar. Making new substances does not add mass (choice 1), no atoms are destroyed (choice 2), and the atoms are still all there, so the mass is not zero (choice 4).

lessons.title: Common Reaction Types, and Reactions Carry Energy
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Reactions are not all the same shape. Some join things together, some split things apart, some burn, and some swap partners. Learning a few common **patterns** helps you recognize what is going on — no memorizing required, just recognizing.

**Build.** Here are four common **reaction types**, with one plain line and an everyday example each.

- **Combination** (also called synthesis): **two things join into one**. Pattern A + B → AB. Everyday: iron joins with oxygen to form rust.
- **Decomposition**: **one thing breaks into two** (or more). Pattern AB → A + B. Everyday: water can be split into hydrogen and oxygen gas.
- **Combustion**: a **fuel reacts with oxygen and releases energy** as heat and light. Everyday: burning wood, gas, or a candle.
- **Replacement**: **atoms swap partners**. In a single replacement one atom takes another's place; in a double replacement two compounds trade partners. We keep this one light — just picture partners switching.

You do not need to drill these. Just notice the pattern: did things join, split, burn, or swap?

Finally, recall how you **recognize a reaction**: color change, gas, heat or light, or a solid (precipitate) forming. Notice that combustion *releases* energy — and that is a clue to a bigger idea. Reactions can either **release energy** (like a fire warming your hands) or **absorb energy** (soaking up heat, like a cold pack). We will explore this fully in the energy module; for now, just hold the thought that **every reaction also has energy going one way or the other**.

**Recap.** Four patterns to recognize: **combination** (join), **decomposition** (split), **combustion** (fuel + oxygen → energy), and **replacement** (swap partners). And reactions either **release or absorb energy** — more on that ahead.

items.prompt: A COMBINATION (synthesis) reaction is one where:
choices: Two things join together into one
One thing breaks apart into two
Atoms swap partners
A fuel burns and disappears
items.explanation: In a combination reaction, two things join into one (A + B → AB), like iron and oxygen forming rust. Breaking one into two is decomposition (choice 2), swapping partners is replacement (choice 3), and burning a fuel is combustion (choice 4).

items.prompt: A DECOMPOSITION reaction is one where:
choices: Two substances join into a single product
One substance breaks apart into two or more
A fuel combines with oxygen to release energy
Nothing changes at all
items.explanation: Decomposition means one substance breaks apart into two or more (AB → A + B), like water splitting into hydrogen and oxygen. Two joining into one is combination (choice 1), fuel plus oxygen is combustion (choice 3), and a reaction does involve change (choice 4).

items.prompt: Burning wood in a fire, where a fuel reacts with oxygen and gives off heat and light, is an example of:
choices: Decomposition
Double replacement
Combustion
Combination only, with no energy released
items.explanation: Combustion is a fuel reacting with oxygen and releasing energy as heat and light — exactly what burning wood does. It is not a simple break-up (decomposition, choice 1) or a partner swap (double replacement, choice 2), and combustion does release energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: Water being split into hydrogen gas and oxygen gas is best described as which reaction type?
choices: Combination, because two things join
Decomposition, because one thing breaks into two
Combustion, because oxygen is involved
Replacement, because partners swap
items.explanation: One substance (water) breaks apart into two (hydrogen and oxygen), which is decomposition. Nothing is joining, so it is not combination (choice 1); no fuel is burning to release energy, so not combustion (choice 3); and no partners trade places, so not replacement (choice 4).

items.prompt: A REPLACEMENT reaction is one where:
choices: Atoms swap partners
One substance simply changes color and nothing else
Two substances join permanently into one
A solid is heated until it glows
items.explanation: In a replacement reaction, atoms swap partners — single replacement (one takes another's place) or double replacement (two compounds trade). A mere color change (choice 2) is not the defining pattern; joining into one is combination (choice 3); and heating a glowing solid (choice 4) is not what replacement means.

items.prompt: The reaction pattern A + B → AB matches which type?
choices: Decomposition
Combination
Combustion
Single replacement
items.explanation: A + B → AB shows two things joining into one, which is the combination pattern. Decomposition is the reverse, AB → A + B (choice 1); combustion is a fuel plus oxygen releasing energy (choice 3); and single replacement involves a partner swap, not a simple join (choice 4).

items.prompt: Besides making new substances, what else is true of chemical reactions in terms of energy?
choices: Reactions never involve energy in any way
Reactions can either release energy or absorb energy
Reactions only ever absorb energy, never release it
Reactions always stay at exactly the same temperature
items.explanation: Reactions either release energy (like a fire warming your hands) or absorb energy (like a cold pack), with the full story coming in the energy module. They do involve energy (choice 1), they can release as well as absorb (choice 3), and they often change temperature (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which list correctly matches the everyday example to its reaction type?
choices: Burning a candle = decomposition; splitting water = combustion
Iron forming rust = combination; burning a candle = combustion
Splitting water = combination; iron forming rust = decomposition
Burning a candle = combination; iron forming rust = combustion
items.explanation: Iron joining with oxygen to form rust is a combination (two join into one), and a burning candle is combustion (fuel + oxygen releasing energy). The other choices mislabel these: splitting water is decomposition and burning is combustion, not the swaps shown.

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up this lesson?
choices: All reactions are exactly the same type and never involve energy
Reactions come in recognizable patterns (combination, decomposition, combustion, replacement) and either release or absorb energy
Only combustion is a real reaction; the others are made up
Reaction types change the atoms into brand-new atoms
items.explanation: Reactions fall into recognizable patterns — combination, decomposition, combustion, and replacement — and they also either release or absorb energy. Reactions are not all one type (choice 1), all four types are real (choice 3), and reactions rearrange existing atoms rather than creating new ones (choice 4).

lessons.title: Solid, Liquid, Gas: It's All About the Particles
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Ice, water, and steam are all the same stuff. So why does one hold its shape, one pours, and one floats away? The answer is hiding in the particles.

**Build.** Remember the **particle view**: all matter is made of tiny bits (**particles**) that are always moving. A **state of matter** is simply *how those particles are arranged and how much they move*. There are three common states.

In a **solid**, the particles are **packed tightly in a fixed arrangement** and only **vibrate in place** (jiggle, without leaving their spots). Because the particles cannot wander, a solid keeps a **fixed shape and a fixed volume** (volume = how much space it takes up). Think of an **ice** cube.

In a **liquid**, the particles are still close together but loose enough to **slide past one another**. So a liquid has a **fixed volume** but **takes the shape of its container** and can **flow**. Think of **water** in a glass.

In a **gas**, the particles are **far apart and zoom around freely**. A gas **fills any container** it is put in and is **easily squashed** into a smaller space (lots of empty room between particles). Think of **steam**.

There is also a fourth state, **plasma**: super-hot gas whose particles have become **charged** — you see it in stars and neon signs. (We will stick to the everyday three.)

**Recap.** Solid = particles packed and only vibrating (fixed shape and volume). Liquid = particles sliding past each other (fixed volume, takes the container's shape, flows). Gas = particles far apart and zooming (fills any container, easily squashed). Plasma is a rare super-hot, charged fourth state.

items.prompt: In which state are the particles packed tightly in a fixed arrangement, only vibrating in place?
choices: Liquid
Solid
Gas
Plasma
items.explanation: Packed tight in fixed spots, only vibrating, describes a solid — which is why a solid keeps its shape. In a liquid the particles slide past each other (choice 1), in a gas they are far apart and zooming (choice 3), and plasma is a super-hot charged gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: In which state are the particles close together but able to slide past one another?
choices: Liquid
Solid
Gas
None — particles never slide
items.explanation: Close together but sliding past each other describes a liquid, which is why a liquid flows and takes its container's shape. A solid's particles are locked in fixed spots (choice 2), a gas's are far apart (choice 3), and particles certainly can slide (choice 4).

items.prompt: A beginner guesses that the particles in a GAS are packed tightly together. What is the correct picture?
choices: Correct — gas particles are squeezed tightly side by side
Gas particles are far apart and zoom around freely
Gas particles are locked in a fixed arrangement
A gas has no particles at all
items.explanation: Gas particles are actually far apart with lots of empty space between them — that is why a gas can be squashed and fills any container. Picturing them packed tight (choice 1) is a common mistake; locked-in arrangement is a solid (choice 3); and a gas is still matter made of particles (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday trio shows solid, liquid, and gas — in that order — of the same substance?
choices: Steam, ice, water
Water, steam, ice
Ice, steam, water
Ice, water, steam
items.explanation: Ice (solid), water (liquid), and steam (gas) are all water in its three states, in that order. The other lists put them out of order, mixing up which everyday form goes with which state.

items.prompt: Why does a gas easily get squashed into a smaller space, while a solid does not?
choices: A gas has lots of empty space between its far-apart particles
A gas weighs nothing, so it cannot resist
A gas has no particles to get in the way
A solid's particles are even farther apart than a gas's
items.explanation: Gas particles are spread far apart with empty room between them, so they can be pushed closer — squashed. The reason is spacing, not weightlessness (choice 2); a gas does have particles (choice 3); and a solid's particles are packed much closer, not farther apart (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which behavior matches a LIQUID?
choices: Keeps a fixed shape no matter what container it is in
Fills any container completely, top to bottom
Keeps a fixed volume but takes the shape of its container and can flow
Cannot move or flow at all
items.explanation: A liquid holds a fixed volume but flows and takes the container's shape — like water poured into a glass. Keeping a fixed shape describes a solid (choice 1), filling any container describes a gas (choice 2), and a liquid clearly can flow (choice 4).

items.prompt: What makes plasma different from an ordinary gas?
choices: Its particles are packed tightly like a solid
It is super-hot, and its particles have become charged
It is colder than ice
It has no particles, only light
items.explanation: Plasma is a super-hot state whose particles have become charged — found in stars and neon signs. Its particles are not packed like a solid (choice 1), it is extremely hot rather than cold (choice 3), and it is still made of particles (choice 4).

items.prompt: A solid keeps both a fixed shape AND a fixed volume. Why?
choices: Its particles are packed in fixed spots and only vibrate, so they cannot rearrange
Its particles are far apart and free to move anywhere
It has no particles to hold a shape
Its particles slide past each other constantly
items.explanation: Because solid particles sit in fixed positions and only vibrate, they cannot move to a new arrangement — so the shape and volume stay put. Far-apart free particles describe a gas (choice 2), a solid does have particles (choice 3), and sliding particles describe a liquid (choice 4).

lessons.title: What Temperature Really Is
lessons.content:
**Hook.** We say a cup of cocoa is 'hot' and an ice pop is 'cold.' But what is actually different inside them? Down at the particle level, the answer is one word: **speed**.

**Build.** All those tiny particles are constantly moving — vibrating, sliding, or zooming, depending on the state. They do not all move at exactly the same speed, so we talk about the **average**. **Temperature** is a measure of the **average motion (energy) of the particles**: the faster they move on average, the **higher** the temperature.

So 'hot' really means the particles are moving fast, and 'cold' means they are moving slowly. This gives us a simple rule for heating and cooling. **Heating** something **gives its particles more energy**, so they speed up. **Cooling** something **takes energy away**, so they slow down. Nothing magical is added or removed except energy of motion.

You feel this every day. Warm a pan and the metal's particles vibrate harder. Heat soup and its particles jiggle and zoom faster, which is why a hot drink steams. Put juice in the freezer and its particles slow right down. The everyday hook: **heat makes the jiggling and zooming speed up; cold makes it slow down.**

This idea is the key to the next lesson. If temperature is just particle speed, then adding or removing energy is the lever that changes a substance from one state to another — melting ice, boiling water, and so on.

**Recap.** Temperature measures the average motion (energy) of particles: hotter means faster, colder means slower. Heating adds energy and speeds particles up; cooling removes energy and slows them down.

items.prompt: In the particle view, temperature is a measure of:
choices: The color of a substance
The average motion (energy) of the particles
How heavy the particles are
How many particles there are
items.explanation: Temperature measures how fast the particles move on average — their average motion or energy. It is not about color (choice 1), the weight of particles (choice 3), or how many there are (choice 4).

items.prompt: If one cup of water is hotter than another, its particles are:
choices: Moving faster on average
Moving slower on average
Completely still
Exactly the same speed as in the cooler cup
items.explanation: Hotter means the particles move faster on average — that is what a higher temperature is. Slower (choice 2) and still (choice 3) describe colder, not hotter, and the same speed (choice 4) would mean the same temperature.

items.prompt: What happens to a substance's particles when you HEAT it?
choices: They lose energy and slow down
They stop moving
They gain energy and speed up
They turn into different particles
items.explanation: Heating gives particles more energy, so they move faster. Losing energy and slowing (choice 1) and stopping (choice 2) describe cooling, and heating does not change what the particles are (choice 4).

items.prompt: Cooling a substance down does what to its particles?
choices: Adds energy so they speed up
Takes energy away so they slow down
Leaves their speed unchanged
Makes them far apart no matter the state
items.explanation: Cooling removes energy, so the particles move more slowly. Adding energy and speeding up (choice 1) is heating, cooling does change their speed (choice 3), and cooling actually tends to bring particles closer, not push them apart (choice 4).

items.prompt: Because we talk about the AVERAGE motion of particles, which statement is most accurate?
choices: Every particle moves at exactly the same speed
The particles move at a range of speeds, and temperature reflects their average
Only one particle moves while the rest stay still
Particles only move when you can see them move
items.explanation: Particles move at a spread of speeds, and temperature captures the average of all that motion. They do not all move identically (choice 1), it is not just one moving (choice 3), and particle motion happens whether or not you can see it (choice 4).

items.prompt: A pot of soup is heated on the stove. In particle terms, what is happening as it warms?
choices: The soup particles jiggle and zoom faster as they gain energy
The soup particles freeze in place
New soup particles are being created
The particles slow down because heat removes energy
items.explanation: Warming the soup adds energy, so its particles move faster — which is why hot soup steams. They do not freeze (choice 2), no new particles appear (choice 3), and heat adds energy rather than removing it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why is 'temperature = average particle motion' the key idea for understanding phase changes?
choices: Because color decides what state something is in
Because adding or removing energy changes particle speed, which can switch a substance between states
Because temperature has nothing to do with particles
Because particles only matter for gases
items.explanation: Since temperature is particle speed, adding or removing energy is exactly the lever that flips a substance between solid, liquid, and gas. Color does not set the state (choice 1), temperature is all about particles (choice 3), and particles matter for every state (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which best matches the everyday hook for what temperature is?
choices: Hot and cold are just feelings with no particle meaning
Cold means the particles move faster than when hot
Temperature only changes the number of particles
Hot means the particles' jiggling and zooming speeds up; cold means it slows down
items.explanation: Heat speeds the jiggling and zooming up; cold slows it down — that is the intuitive picture. Temperature does have a real particle meaning (choice 1), cold means slower not faster (choice 2), and it changes their speed, not their number (choice 3).

lessons.title: Phase Changes: Adding and Removing Energy
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Ice melts in your hand, a puddle dries up in the sun, and fog appears on a cold window. These are all the same kind of event: a **phase change**.

**Build.** A **phase change** is when a substance switches from one state to another. From the last lesson we know the lever: **adding energy** (heating) drives a substance 'up' toward gas, and **removing energy** (cooling) drives it 'down' toward solid. Each phase change has a plain name.

Adding energy (moving up):
- **Melting**: solid → liquid. Ice melting into water.
- **Boiling / evaporation**: liquid → gas. A pot of water boiling away; a puddle drying.
- **Sublimation**: solid → gas **directly**, skipping the liquid. Dry ice 'smoking', or ice cubes slowly shrinking in the freezer.

Removing energy (moving down):
- **Condensation**: gas → liquid. Dew on grass, fog on a cold window.
- **Freezing**: liquid → solid. Water turning to ice.

Notice the pairs are opposites: melting undoes freezing, boiling undoes condensation. A common trap is to think **sublimation** must pass through liquid — it does not; the solid goes straight to gas (that is the whole point of the word). Another trap is mixing up **melting** (solid becoming liquid) with **freezing** (liquid becoming solid) — remember melting needs *added* heat, freezing needs heat *removed*.

**Recap.** Phase changes are switches between states driven by energy. Adding energy: melting (solid→liquid), boiling/evaporation (liquid→gas), sublimation (solid→gas directly). Removing energy: condensation (gas→liquid), freezing (liquid→solid).

items.prompt: What do we call a change of solid directly into gas, skipping the liquid stage?
choices: Condensation
Melting
Sublimation
Freezing
items.explanation: Solid going straight to gas (like dry ice) is sublimation. Condensation is gas to liquid (choice 1), melting is solid to liquid (choice 2), and freezing is liquid to solid (choice 4).

items.prompt: Dew forming on grass, or fog on a cold window, is an example of:
choices: Condensation (gas turning into liquid)
Sublimation (solid turning into gas)
Melting (solid turning into liquid)
Boiling (liquid turning into gas)
items.explanation: Water vapor in the air cools and turns into liquid droplets — gas to liquid is condensation. Sublimation is solid to gas (choice 2), melting is solid to liquid (choice 3), and boiling is liquid to gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which phase change requires ADDING energy?
choices: Freezing (liquid → solid)
Condensation (gas → liquid)
Melting (solid → liquid)
All phase changes remove energy
items.explanation: Melting needs added energy to free the particles from their fixed spots, moving 'up' toward gas. Freezing (choice 1) and condensation (choice 2) both release/remove energy, and not all phase changes remove energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student thinks sublimation goes solid → liquid → gas, passing through liquid. Why is that wrong?
choices: Sublimation actually goes gas → solid
Sublimation goes solid straight to gas, skipping the liquid entirely
Sublimation only happens to liquids
Sublimation is just another word for melting
items.explanation: The whole idea of sublimation is that the solid turns directly into gas with no liquid in between — like dry ice. It is not gas to solid (choice 1), it starts from a solid not a liquid (choice 3), and it is not the same as melting (choice 4).

items.prompt: Water turning into ice in the freezer is called:
choices: Melting
Boiling
Sublimation
Freezing
items.explanation: Liquid water losing energy and becoming solid ice is freezing. Melting is the reverse, solid to liquid (choice 1); boiling is liquid to gas (choice 2); and sublimation is solid straight to gas (choice 3).

items.prompt: Someone mixes up melting and freezing. Which statement sets it straight?
choices: Melting is liquid → solid; freezing is solid → liquid
Melting is solid → liquid (add heat); freezing is liquid → solid (remove heat)
Melting and freezing are the same change
Both melting and freezing turn a liquid into a gas
items.explanation: Melting takes a solid to a liquid by adding heat; freezing takes a liquid to a solid by removing heat — they are opposites. Choice 1 reverses both directions, choice 3 wrongly calls them the same, and neither one makes a gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: Dry ice appears to 'smoke' as it turns directly from solid to a gas. This is an everyday example of:
choices: Freezing
Condensation
Sublimation
Melting
items.explanation: Dry ice going straight from solid to gas is sublimation. Freezing makes a solid from a liquid (choice 1), condensation makes a liquid from a gas (choice 2), and melting makes a liquid from a solid (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which pairing of opposite phase changes is correct?
choices: Melting is undone by boiling
Boiling is undone by condensation
Freezing is undone by sublimation
Condensation is undone by freezing
items.explanation: Boiling (liquid→gas) and condensation (gas→liquid) are exact opposites. Melting is undone by freezing, not boiling (choice 1); freezing is undone by melting, not sublimation (choice 3); and condensation is undone by boiling/evaporation, not freezing (choice 4).

lessons.title: Melting Points, the Heating-Curve Pause, and Water's Surprise
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Put a thermometer in a pot of ice water and start heating. The temperature climbs… then it stops climbing while the ice melts, even though the flame is still on. Why does the temperature take a break?

**Build.** First, two useful facts. Every substance changes state at its own **characteristic temperatures**. The **melting point** is the temperature where it melts (and freezes); the **boiling point** is where it boils (and condenses). For water these are fixed: it melts at **0°C** and boils at **100°C**. These are reliable **properties** of the substance.

Now the subtle, important idea. While a substance is **changing state**, the energy you add does **not** raise the temperature. Instead it goes into **breaking the attractions between particles** — pulling them apart so they can move into the new arrangement. Only once the change is finished does added energy start speeding the particles up again. So on a **heating curve** (temperature versus heat added), the line **rises, then pauses flat during melting, rises again, then pauses flat during boiling**. Everyday hook: a glass of ice water stays at 0°C until all the ice has melted, and a pot of boiling water stays around 100°C no matter how high you turn the flame.

Finally, water's oddity. **Most substances shrink when they solidify**, but **water expands when it freezes** — so ice is less dense and **floats** (and freezing water can burst pipes), because of how its molecules line up.

**Recap.** Substances change state at characteristic melting and boiling points (water: 0°C and 100°C). During a phase change the temperature pauses, because added energy breaks attractions between particles instead of heating them. And unusually, water expands when it freezes, so ice floats.

items.prompt: At what temperatures does water melt and boil?
choices: Melts at 100°C, boils at 0°C
Melts at 0°C, boils at 100°C
Melts at 50°C, boils at 200°C
Water has no fixed melting or boiling point
items.explanation: Water melts at 0°C and boils at 100°C — fixed, characteristic properties. Choice 1 swaps the two, choice 3 gives wrong values, and water does have fixed points (choice 4).

items.prompt: While a substance is melting, what happens to its temperature?
choices: It keeps rising quickly the whole time
It drops sharply
It rises and falls randomly
It pauses and stays flat until the melting is done
items.explanation: During melting the temperature pauses on a flat line until all the solid has melted. It does not keep rising (choice 1), drop (choice 2), or wobble randomly (choice 3) — the added energy is busy with the change, not the temperature.

items.prompt: Why does the temperature pause during melting or boiling, even while you keep adding heat?
choices: Because the thermometer stops working at those temperatures
Because no energy is being added at that moment
Because the added energy goes into breaking the attractions between particles, not into raising the temperature
Because the particles stop moving completely
items.explanation: During a phase change the energy you add is used to pull particles apart (break their attractions), so the temperature holds steady until the change is complete. The thermometer is fine (choice 1), energy is still being added (choice 2), and the particles keep moving (choice 4).

items.prompt: A beginner thinks the temperature must keep rising during boiling because the flame is on. Why is that wrong?
choices: It is right — boiling water gets hotter and hotter without limit
Boiling water stays around 100°C; the extra energy goes into turning liquid into gas, not raising the temperature
Boiling water actually cools down the longer it boils
The flame adds no energy at all to boiling water
items.explanation: A pot of boiling water holds near 100°C no matter how high the flame, because the energy is spent converting liquid to gas. It does not climb without limit (choice 1) or cool down (choice 3), and the flame is certainly adding energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: On a heating curve (temperature versus heat added), the flat pauses appear:
choices: At the start and end, but never during a state change
During melting and during boiling, while the substance changes state
Only after the substance has fully become a gas
Never — the line always rises steadily
items.explanation: The line goes flat during melting and during boiling — exactly when the substance is changing state. The pauses are not at the ends only, never during a change (choice 1), and not only after becoming gas (choice 3); the line does flatten, so it does not always rise steadily (choice 4).

items.prompt: What is unusual about water when it freezes?
choices: It expands, so ice is less dense and floats
It shrinks, like most substances do when they solidify
It disappears completely
It gets heavier without changing volume
items.explanation: Water expands as it freezes, so ice floats and freezing water can burst pipes — unusual, since most things shrink when they solidify. It does not shrink like most substances (choice 2), disappear (choice 3), or just gain weight (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why does ice float on liquid water?
choices: Ice is denser than the water around it
Water expands when it freezes, making ice less dense than liquid water
Ice has no mass at all
Ice is warmer than the water it sits on
items.explanation: Because water expands on freezing, the same amount of water takes up more space as ice, making it less dense — so it floats. Ice is less dense, not denser (choice 1); it does have mass (choice 3); and it is not warmer than the liquid (choice 4).

items.prompt: An ice-water mixture is heated slowly. What temperature does it stay at until all the ice has melted?
choices: It keeps climbing past 0°C right away
It jumps straight to 100°C
It drops below 0°C
It stays at about 0°C until the ice is fully melted
items.explanation: The mixture holds near 0°C until the last of the ice melts, because the energy is going into the melting, not into raising the temperature. It does not climb immediately (choice 1), jump to 100°C (choice 2), or drop lower (choice 3).

lessons.title: What a Gas Is, and What Pressure Really Is
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Air, a party balloon, the helium that makes it float, the smell of dinner drifting across a room — these are all gases at work. From the last topic you already know the big picture: a gas is **tiny particles, far apart, zooming and bouncing around**. Let us build one new idea on top of that: **pressure**.

**Build.** First, a quick recap of what makes a gas a gas. Its particles are spread far apart, with lots of empty space between them, **zooming and bouncing off each other and off the walls of their container**. Because there is so much empty room, a gas **spreads to fill any space** you put it in, and it is **easily squeezed (compressed)** into a smaller space. (Compare a solid, whose particles are packed tight and cannot be squashed.)

Now the new idea. Those zooming particles are constantly **slamming into the walls** of whatever holds them. **Pressure** is the **total push from all those tiny collisions on the walls**. You cannot feel one particle, but billions of hits add up to a steady push. **More hits, or harder hits, mean more pressure.** That is the whole idea — pressure is collisions on the walls, nothing more mysterious.

You meet this every day. A pumped-up **tire** or a blown-up **balloon** feels firm because so many particles are hitting the inside walls. Air pressure pushing on a balloon from inside is what holds its shape. Let the air out and the hits stop — the balloon goes limp.

**Recap.** A gas is far-apart, fast particles that fill any space and squeeze easily. **Pressure is the total push from all those particle collisions on the container walls** — more or harder hits, more pressure. Next we will see what happens when you squeeze a gas, heat it, or add more of it.

items.prompt: What is gas pressure, in the particle view?
choices: The total push from all the gas particles colliding with the container walls
The weight of the empty container by itself
The color of the gas inside the container
Something gases simply do not have
items.explanation: Pressure is the combined push of countless tiny particle collisions on the walls — more hits or harder hits mean more pressure. It is not the container's weight (choice 2) or its color (choice 3), and gases certainly do have pressure (choice 4): thinking pressure is unrelated to collisions is the misconception to avoid.

items.prompt: Which best describes the particles in a gas?
choices: Packed tightly in fixed spots, only vibrating
Close together but sliding past one another
Far apart, zooming and bouncing off each other and the walls
Frozen perfectly still
items.explanation: Gas particles are far apart with lots of empty space, zooming and bouncing around — that is why a gas fills any space and squeezes easily. Packed-and-vibrating (choice 1) describes a solid, sliding-but-close (choice 2) describes a liquid, and gas particles are always moving, never still (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why can a gas be squeezed (compressed) into a smaller space, while a solid cannot?
choices: A gas weighs nothing, so it offers no resistance
A gas has lots of empty space between its far-apart particles
A gas has no particles at all to get in the way
Gases actually cannot be compressed
items.explanation: Because gas particles are far apart with empty room between them, they can be pushed closer together. It is about spacing, not weightlessness (choice 1); a gas does have particles (choice 3); and gases very much can be compressed — believing they cannot (choice 4) is a common mistake.

items.prompt: A blown-up balloon feels firm. In particle terms, why?
choices: The rubber alone is stiff; the air inside does nothing
The gas particles inside keep hitting the walls, and all those hits push outward
There is a solid block of gas pressing on the walls
Cold has frozen the balloon into shape
items.explanation: The firmness comes from countless gas particles striking the inner walls — that push is pressure. The air is doing the work, not the rubber alone (choice 1); a gas is not a packed solid block (choice 3); and the firmness is from collisions, not freezing (choice 4).

items.prompt: What does it mean to say a gas 'fills any container' it is put in?
choices: The gas sinks to the bottom and stays in a small puddle
The gas keeps a fixed shape no matter the container
The gas can only exist in a perfectly round container
The gas spreads out until its particles reach every part of the space
items.explanation: Because its particles zoom freely with lots of empty space, a gas spreads to occupy the whole container. It does not pool at the bottom like a liquid (choice 1), it has no fixed shape of its own (choice 2), and the container's shape does not matter (choice 3).

items.prompt: If MORE particles hit the walls (or they hit harder), what happens to the pressure?
choices: The pressure goes down
The pressure stays exactly the same
The pressure goes up
Pressure does not depend on how the particles hit
items.explanation: More hits, or harder hits, add up to a bigger total push — so pressure rises. It does not fall (choice 1) or stay fixed (choice 2), and pressure depends entirely on those collisions — thinking otherwise (choice 4) is the misconception to drop.

items.prompt: You let the air out of a balloon and it goes limp. Why?
choices: The particles speed up and push harder
Fewer particles are left inside, so there are fewer wall-hits and less pressure
The rubber turns into a gas
Letting air out makes the pressure inside increase
items.explanation: With most of the gas gone, far fewer particles remain to strike the walls, so the outward push (pressure) drops and the balloon sags. The particles are not pushing harder (choice 1), the rubber does not become gas (choice 3), and removing gas lowers pressure, not raises it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday observation is the best example of a gas filling and spreading through a space?
choices: A rock keeping its exact shape on a shelf
Water staying in the bottom of a glass
The smell of cooking slowly spreading to fill a whole room
An ice cube holding firm in your hand
items.explanation: A smell spreading to fill a room is gas particles zooming out to occupy all the available space. A rock (choice 1) is a solid with fixed shape, water in a glass (choice 2) is a liquid that pools, and an ice cube (choice 4) is a solid — none of them spread to fill a room.

lessons.title: Squeeze It, Heat It, Add More: Three Ways to Change Pressure
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now that pressure means 'particles hitting the walls,' three everyday tricks all make sense. Squeeze a gas, heat a gas, or add more gas — each one changes how often the particles hit the walls, so each one changes the pressure. Let us take them one at a time, and give the **cause** every time.

**Build.** **1) Squeeze it (less volume → more pressure).** Push the plunger of a blocked syringe in. You have not removed any particles — you have just given them **less room**. In a smaller space they reach the walls sooner and hit them **more often**, so the pressure rises. The cause: same particles, smaller space, more frequent hits. (This is **Boyle's idea**, in plain words.) One clean number: **halve the volume and the pressure roughly doubles.** Hook: a blocked syringe gets harder to push the more you squeeze; a squeezed balloon bulges.

**2) Heat it (faster particles → more pressure, or it expands).** Remember temperature = how fast the particles move. **Heat a gas and its particles speed up.** Faster particles hit the walls **more often and harder**. If the container is sealed (fixed size), the **pressure rises**. If the container can stretch, the gas **expands** instead. (This is the **Charles / Gay-Lussac idea**.) Hooks: a tire or sealed can heats up in the sun; a balloon swells in heat and shrinks in the cold.

**3) Add more gas (more particles → more pressure).** Pump more air into a tire. **More particles** in the same space means **more collisions** with the walls, so the pressure climbs. Hook: each pump makes the tire firmer.

These three cause-and-effect rules are exactly what the **gas laws** summarize — but you do not need any formula to see why they are true.

**Recap.** Squeeze (less volume) → more frequent hits → more pressure (halve volume ≈ double pressure). Heat → faster particles → more pressure (sealed) or expansion (stretchy). Add gas → more particles → more hits → more pressure.

items.prompt: You squeeze a gas into a smaller space (less volume) without removing any particles. What happens to the pressure?
choices: It goes down, because squeezing relaxes the gas
It rises, because the same particles now hit the walls more often
It stays exactly the same
Nothing — a gas cannot be squeezed at all
items.explanation: Same particles in a smaller space reach the walls sooner and hit them more frequently, so pressure rises. Thinking squeezing lowers pressure (choice 1) is a classic misconception; the pressure does change (choice 3); and gases can indeed be compressed (choice 4).

items.prompt: About how much does the pressure change if you HALVE the volume of a gas (keeping everything else the same)?
choices: It roughly doubles
It roughly halves
It drops to zero
It does not change
items.explanation: Halving the space packs the same particles into half the room, so they hit the walls about twice as often — the pressure roughly doubles. It does not halve (choice 2), fall to zero (choice 3), or stay put (choice 4); smaller volume means higher pressure.

items.prompt: Why does pushing the plunger of a blocked syringe get harder the more you squeeze?
choices: The gas particles vanish as you push
The gas turns into a liquid right away
As the space shrinks, the particles hit the plunger more often, pushing back with more pressure
Squeezing lowers the pressure, which feels harder
items.explanation: Shrinking the space makes the trapped particles strike the plunger more frequently, so they push back harder — higher pressure resists you. The particles do not vanish (choice 1) or simply become liquid (choice 2), and squeezing raises pressure, not lowers it (choice 4).

items.prompt: You heat a gas inside a SEALED, fixed-size container. What happens, and why?
choices: The pressure drops, because heat calms the particles down
Nothing changes, because heat does not affect a gas
The particles slow down, so the pressure falls
The particles speed up and hit the walls more often and harder, so the pressure rises
items.explanation: Heating speeds the particles up; faster particles hit the walls more often and harder, so in a sealed container the pressure rises. Heating does not calm or slow particles (choices 1 and 3) — thinking heat lowers pressure is the misconception — and heat clearly does affect a gas (choice 2).

items.prompt: When you heat a gas, what is the underlying cause of the change?
choices: The particles slow down and bunch up
New gas particles are created out of the heat
The particles stop hitting the walls entirely
The particles speed up (higher temperature means faster motion)
items.explanation: Temperature is particle speed, so heating makes the particles move faster — that faster motion is what drives more pressure or expansion. Heat does not slow them (choice 1), create new particles (choice 2), or stop the collisions (choice 3).

items.prompt: A balloon that can stretch is warmed up. Instead of the pressure rising a lot, what tends to happen?
choices: The balloon shrinks as it warms
The balloon expands, because the faster particles push the stretchy walls outward
The balloon stays exactly the same size
The gas inside turns solid
items.explanation: If the walls can move, the faster, harder-hitting particles push them outward, so the balloon expands rather than building huge pressure. A warmed balloon swells, not shrinks (choice 1) or stays fixed (choice 3), and the gas does not turn solid (choice 4).

items.prompt: You pump MORE air into a tire that stays the same size. What happens to the pressure, and why?
choices: It falls, because more particles share the push
It stays the same, since the tire is the same size
It rises, because more particles mean more collisions with the walls
It rises only if you also cool the tire
items.explanation: Adding particles to the same space means more of them strike the walls, so the total push — the pressure — climbs. More gas raises pressure; it does not lower it (choice 1) or leave it unchanged (choice 2), and it works regardless of cooling (choice 4).

items.prompt: A balloon left outside on a very cold day looks smaller and droopier than it did indoors. Why?
choices: Cold makes the particles speed up, raising pressure
The cold particles move slower and hit the walls less, so the gas takes up less space
Some of the rubber evaporated in the cold
Cold creates extra gas particles inside
items.explanation: In the cold the particles slow down and hit the walls less often and more softly, so the gas contracts and the balloon shrinks. Cold slows particles, it does not speed them up (choice 1); the rubber does not evaporate (choice 3); and cold does not create particles (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which set of cause-and-effect rules is correct?
choices: Less volume → less pressure; more heat → less pressure; more gas → less pressure
Less volume → more pressure; more heat → more pressure or expansion; more gas → more pressure
Less volume → no change; more heat → no change; more gas → no change
Only adding more gas changes pressure; squeezing and heating do nothing
items.explanation: Squeezing (less volume), heating, and adding gas each make particles hit the walls more, so each raises pressure (or, for a stretchy container, causes expansion). Choice 1 reverses every effect, choice 3 wrongly says nothing changes, and choice 4 ignores squeezing and heating.

lessons.title: Gases in Everyday Life: Safety, Balloons, and Spreading Out
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Spray cans warn 'do not store near heat.' Tires are checked on hot days. A balloon left in a hot car can pop. These are not random rules — they all come straight from how gas particles behave. Let us turn the ideas into everyday sense and one important **safety** point.

**Build.** The key danger is a **sealed container of gas that gets heated.** A sealed container cannot grow, so when you heat the gas inside, the particles speed up and hit the walls harder and harder — the **pressure keeps climbing**. If the walls cannot take it, the container **bursts**. That is why you must **never throw an aerosol (spray) can into a fire** and never leave sealed pressurized containers in strong heat: the building pressure can make them explode. This is the single most important gas-safety idea: **sealed gas + heat = rising pressure = burst risk.**

Stretchy and everyday objects show the gentler version. A **balloon pops in the heat** because the warm gas expands and pushes the rubber past its limit; the same balloon **deflates and droops in the cold** because the gas contracts. A **tire** reads higher pressure on a hot day and lower when it is cold. Bubbles, breathing (your lungs draw air in and push it out), and even weather all involve gases moving and changing pressure.

Finally, a light recap of **diffusion**: because gas particles are always moving and far apart, a gas **spreads out to fill a space and mixes** with the air — which is why a smell from the kitchen reaches the far side of the house. It ties back to the particle picture: constant motion carries the particles everywhere.

**Recap.** A gas is far-apart fast particles; pressure is their collisions on the walls. **Squeeze it → more pressure; heat it → more pressure or it expands; add more gas → more pressure.** That explains tires, balloons, and spray cans — and warns us that **heating a sealed gas container is dangerous** because the pressure builds until it can burst. And because the particles never stop moving, gases spread out and mix (diffusion).

items.prompt: Why is heating a SEALED container of gas dangerous?
choices: Heat removes the gas, leaving a harmless empty can
The gas cannot expand, so the pressure keeps rising until the container can burst
Heating a sealed gas always makes it perfectly safe
Heat lowers the pressure, which weakens the can harmlessly
items.explanation: In a sealed container the gas cannot expand, so heating speeds the particles up and the pressure climbs until the walls may fail and burst. Heat does not remove the gas (choice 1); a sealed heated gas is not safe (choice 3) — assuming it is is a dangerous misconception; and heat raises pressure, not lowers it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why should you never throw an aerosol (spray) can into a fire?
choices: The fire will simply put itself out
The can is sealed, so the heated gas inside builds pressure and the can can explode
Aerosol cans get colder in a fire
The gas inside cannot be affected by heat
items.explanation: A spray can is a sealed pressurized container; in a fire the gas heats, its pressure rises with nowhere to go, and the can can violently burst. The fire is not put out (choice 1), the can heats rather than cools (choice 3), and the gas is very much affected by heat (choice 4).

items.prompt: A balloon left in a hot car pops. What is the best explanation?
choices: The cold shrank the gas until the balloon tore
The rubber turned into gas
The warm gas expanded, pushing the stretchy rubber past its limit
The gas particles stopped moving, crushing the balloon
items.explanation: Heat speeds the particles up so the gas expands, stretching the rubber until it tears — a pop. It is heat, not cold, doing this (choice 1); the rubber does not become gas (choice 2); and the particles speed up, they do not stop (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why does a balloon deflate and droop when taken outside on a freezing day?
choices: The cold gas contracts as its particles slow down and hit the walls less
The cold adds more gas particles inside
The cold makes the particles speed up and expand
Cold has no effect on a gas
items.explanation: Cold slows the particles, so they hit the walls less often and more softly and the gas takes up less space — the balloon shrinks. Cold does not add particles (choice 2) or speed them up (choice 3), and it clearly does affect the gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: A car tire often reads a higher pressure after a long, hot drive. Why?
choices: Driving removes air from the tire
The air inside warmed up; faster particles hit the walls harder, raising the pressure
Heat makes the gas disappear, raising pressure
The pressure reading is always wrong when warm
items.explanation: The air inside heats during the drive, the particles speed up, and they strike the walls more often and harder — so the pressure rises. Driving does not remove air (choice 1), heat does not make gas disappear (choice 3), and the reading is genuinely higher, not faulty (choice 4).

items.prompt: The smell of cooking reaches the far side of the house. What is this spreading-out of a gas called, and why does it happen?
choices: Condensation, because the gas turns to liquid
Diffusion, because the constantly-moving particles spread out and mix with the air
Freezing, because the gas cools as it travels
Compression, because the gas is being squeezed
items.explanation: Gas particles are always moving and far apart, so they spread out and mix through the air — that is diffusion. It is not condensation (choice 1) or freezing (choice 3), which are phase changes, and nothing is squeezing the gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which precaution follows directly from 'sealed gas plus heat builds pressure'?
choices: Store spray cans and pressurized containers away from heat and never near fire
Keep spray cans as close to a heat source as possible
Heat sealed gas containers to make them safer
Pressurized containers are safe at any temperature
items.explanation: Because heat makes a sealed gas's pressure climb toward bursting, you keep such containers away from heat and fire. Putting them near heat (choice 2) or deliberately heating them (choice 3) is exactly the danger, and they are not safe at any temperature (choice 4) — that is the misconception to avoid.

items.prompt: Pulling all of it together, which statement is fully correct?
choices: Pressure is unrelated to particle collisions, and heating a sealed gas lowers its pressure
Gases cannot be squeezed, and a sealed gas is safe to heat
Pressure is particle collisions on the walls; squeezing or heating or adding gas raises it, so heating a sealed container risks a burst
Squeezing a gas lowers its pressure, and gases never spread out
items.explanation: Pressure is the push of particle collisions on the walls; squeezing, heating, or adding gas all increase it, which is why heating a sealed container is dangerous. Choice 1 wrongly divorces pressure from collisions and reverses the heat effect, choice 2 wrongly says gases cannot be squeezed and that sealed heating is safe, and choice 4 wrongly says squeezing lowers pressure and that gases never diffuse.

lessons.title: Mixtures and Solutions: Stuff Combined, Not Bonded
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Stir some sugar into a cup of tea and it seems to vanish, yet toss a salad and you can still spot every tomato and leaf. Both are 'two things combined,' but they look very different. Why?

**Build.** Earlier we met the idea of a **mixture**. Let us sharpen it. A **mixture** is **two or more substances physically combined** — just mixed together, **not chemically bonded** into a new substance. Because nothing is bonded, each part keeps being itself and a mixture can be **separated** again. Salt mixed into water is still salt and still water.

Mixtures come in two styles. A **homogeneous** mixture is **uniform**: it looks the **same throughout**, so you cannot pick out the parts. Salt water and the air you breathe are homogeneous — every sip or breath is the same blend. A **heterogeneous** mixture is **non-uniform**: you can **see the different parts**. A salad, sand stirred into water, and trail mix are heterogeneous — the pieces stay visibly separate.

One homogeneous mixture is special enough to get its own name: a **solution** is **one substance dissolved evenly in another**. In a solution, the **solute** is what dissolves — usually the **smaller** amount, like the salt or sugar. The **solvent** is what does the dissolving — usually the **larger** amount, very often water. So in salt water, salt is the **solute** and water is the **solvent**, and the two together make a **solution**.

Watch one trap: a solution is **not** a chemical compound. The solute is not bonded to the solvent — it is only mixed in evenly, so you could get it back out.

**Recap.** A mixture is substances physically combined (not bonded), so it can be separated. **Homogeneous** = uniform (salt water, air); **heterogeneous** = you see the parts (a salad). A **solution** is a uniform mixture of a **solute** dissolved evenly in a **solvent** (salt in water).

items.prompt: A mixture is best described as:
choices: Two or more substances physically combined, not bonded, so they can be separated
Two or more substances chemically bonded into a brand-new substance
A single pure substance with only one kind of particle
Anything that looks cloudy
items.explanation: In a mixture the substances are just physically combined, not bonded, so each keeps its identity and can be separated again. Bonding into a new substance (choice 2) is a chemical compound, not a mixture; a single kind of particle (choice 3) is a pure substance; and looking cloudy (choice 4) does not define a mixture.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a HOMOGENEOUS and a HETEROGENEOUS mixture?
choices: Homogeneous means you can see separate parts; heterogeneous means it looks the same throughout
Homogeneous means it looks the same throughout; heterogeneous means you can see different parts
Homogeneous mixtures are bonded; heterogeneous mixtures are not
There is no difference; the words mean the same thing
items.explanation: Homogeneous means uniform — the same throughout (like salt water); heterogeneous means non-uniform, with visibly different parts (like a salad). Choice 1 swaps the two definitions. Neither kind is bonded (choice 3) — both are mixtures — and the words clearly differ (choice 4).

items.prompt: Classify this mixture: a tossed SALAD with lettuce, tomato, and croutons you can clearly see.
choices: Homogeneous, because it is in one bowl
A pure substance, because it is food
Heterogeneous, because you can see the different parts
A solution, because the parts are dissolved
items.explanation: You can see the separate pieces, so the salad is non-uniform — heterogeneous. Being in one bowl (choice 1) does not make it uniform; it is a mixture, not a pure substance (choice 2); and nothing is dissolved, so it is not a solution (choice 4).

items.prompt: Classify this mixture: a glass of clear SALT WATER that looks exactly the same from top to bottom.
choices: Heterogeneous, because salt and water are different things
A pure substance, because it looks clear
Homogeneous, because it looks the same throughout
Not a mixture at all
items.explanation: Salt water looks the same everywhere — uniform — so it is homogeneous. It does not show visibly separate parts, so it is not heterogeneous (choice 1); looking clear does not make it pure, since it has two kinds of stuff (choice 2); and it is definitely a mixture (choice 4).

items.prompt: A SOLUTION is:
choices: A mixture where you can always see the separate parts
A chemical compound formed by bonding two substances together
Any liquid, whether anything is dissolved in it or not
One substance dissolved evenly in another (a uniform mixture)
items.explanation: A solution is a uniform mixture: one substance dissolved evenly in another, like salt in water. You cannot see the separate parts (choice 1) — that would be heterogeneous; it is not bonded, so it is not a compound (choice 2); and a plain liquid with nothing dissolved in it is not a solution (choice 3).

items.prompt: In salt water, which part is the SOLUTE and which is the SOLVENT?
choices: Salt is the solvent; water is the solute
Salt is the solute; water is the solvent
Both salt and water are solutes
Both salt and water are solvents
items.explanation: The solute is what dissolves (the smaller amount) and the solvent is what does the dissolving (the larger amount), so salt is the solute and water is the solvent. Choice 1 reverses the two — a very common mix-up. A solution has one solute and one solvent here, not two of either (choices 3 and 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'When salt dissolves in water, the salt and water bond together into a new compound.' What is the correct fix?
choices: The student is right; a solution is a chemical compound
A solution is just a mixture — the salt is dissolved in, not bonded, so you could get it back
The salt is destroyed and no longer exists
The salt turns into water
items.explanation: A solution is a mixture, not a compound: the solute is spread evenly through the solvent but not bonded to it, so it can be recovered. Calling it a compound is the classic error (choice 1); the salt is not destroyed (choice 3); and it does not become water (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday item is a SOLUTION (a uniform mixture with something dissolved in it)?
choices: Sand stirred into water that settles to the bottom
A bowl of trail mix
A salad with visible vegetables
Sugar fully dissolved in a glass of tea
items.explanation: Sugar dissolved evenly in tea is a uniform mixture — a solution. Sand that settles (choice 1), trail mix (choice 2), and a salad (choice 3) are heterogeneous mixtures where you can see or separate the parts, so they are not solutions.

items.prompt: Why can a mixture like salt water be separated back into salt and water?
choices: Because the parts are physically combined, not bonded, so each keeps its identity
Because the salt and water are chemically bonded together
Because separating destroys the salt
Because salt water is a pure substance
items.explanation: In a mixture the parts are only physically combined, so they keep their own identity and can be pulled apart again. They are not bonded (choice 2); separating recovers the salt rather than destroying it (choice 3); and salt water is a mixture, not a pure substance (choice 4).

lessons.title: Why Things Dissolve, and the Limit on Dissolving
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Salt disappears into water in seconds, but pour in cooking oil and it just floats in a slick on top, refusing to mix. Same water, very different results. What decides whether something dissolves?

**Build.** The handy rule is **'like dissolves like.'** Recall that water is **polar** — its molecule has a slightly negative side and a slightly positive side. Polar water dissolves other things that are **polar or charged**, such as salt and sugar, because the similar, charged parts attract and the water can pull them apart and surround them. But water does **not** dissolve **nonpolar** things like oil, which have no charged sides to grab. So **similar types dissolve each other**, while very different types stay apart — 'oil and water don't mix.' (This is why greasy dishes need soap: soap acts as a go-between for the two.) Keep it simple: like dissolves like.

Now the second idea: there is a **limit** to how much will dissolve. **Solubility** is **how much of a solute can dissolve in a given solvent**. Keep stirring sugar into a glass of tea and at some point no more will dissolve — the extra just sinks and sits at the bottom. A solution that is 'full' like this, holding all the solute it can, is **saturated**. No more solute will dissolve once you reach that point.

Temperature shifts the limit. For most **solids**, **heating lets more dissolve** — sugar dissolves faster and in greater amount in hot tea than in iced tea. For **gases** it is the **opposite**: a gas is **less** soluble when warm, which is why a warm soda goes flat as its dissolved gas escapes.

**Recap.** 'Like dissolves like': polar water dissolves polar/charged things (salt, sugar) but not nonpolar oil. **Solubility** is how much will dissolve, and a **saturated** solution is full — no more dissolves. Heating usually dissolves more **solid**, but less **gas**.

items.prompt: The rule 'like dissolves like' means:
choices: Any substance will dissolve in any other substance
Substances of similar type (such as polar with polar) tend to dissolve each other
Only solids can dissolve, never liquids or gases
Things dissolve only if you like the taste
items.explanation: 'Like dissolves like' means similar types mix — polar water dissolves polar or charged things like salt. It does not mean anything dissolves in anything (choice 1); liquids and gases can dissolve too (choice 3); and taste has nothing to do with it (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why does salt dissolve in water, but oil does not?
choices: Water is polar, so it dissolves polar/charged salt, but not nonpolar oil
Oil is heavier than salt, so it cannot dissolve
Salt is a liquid and oil is a solid
Water dissolves everything except things that are blue
items.explanation: Polar water dissolves salt because salt is polar/charged (like dissolves like), but oil is nonpolar with no charged sides for water to grab, so it separates and floats. Weight (choice 2) is not the reason; salt is a solid and oil a liquid, the reverse of choice 3; and color (choice 4) is irrelevant.

items.prompt: You shake oil and water together hard, then let the glass sit. What happens?
choices: The oil dissolves completely and disappears
The oil and water bond into a new compound
The water turns into oil
The oil and water separate again, with the oil floating on top
items.explanation: Oil is nonpolar and does not dissolve in polar water, so once you stop shaking it separates out and floats — 'oil and water don't mix.' It does not dissolve (choice 1), it does not bond into a compound (choice 2), and water does not become oil (choice 3).

items.prompt: What does SOLUBILITY mean?
choices: How fast a liquid pours
How much of a solute can dissolve in a given solvent
The color a solution turns when you stir it
The total weight of the glass and the solution
items.explanation: Solubility is how much of a solute can dissolve in a given solvent — the size of the dissolving limit. It is not about pouring speed (choice 1), color (choice 3), or the weight of the container (choice 4).

items.prompt: You keep stirring sugar into iced tea until extra sugar just sits at the bottom and will not dissolve. The solution is now:
choices: Saturated — it is full and holds all the sugar it can
Empty, because no sugar is dissolved
A pure substance, because it has lots of sugar
Unsaturated, because more will always dissolve
items.explanation: When no more will dissolve and the extra settles out, the solution is saturated — 'full.' Plenty of sugar is dissolved, so it is not empty (choice 2); it is a mixture, not a pure substance (choice 3); and a saturated solution will NOT take more, so it is not unsaturated (choice 4).

items.prompt: A student says, 'You can dissolve any amount of sugar in a glass of water if you just keep adding it.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because sugar never dissolves in water at all
Because there is a solubility limit — once saturated, no more dissolves and the extra settles out
Because adding sugar turns the water into a solid
Because only salt can dissolve, not sugar
items.explanation: There is a limit to how much will dissolve; once the solution is saturated, extra sugar just piles at the bottom. Sugar does dissolve (choice 1), adding it does not make a solid (choice 3), and sugar dissolves well, not only salt (choice 4). The error is thinking any amount dissolves with no limit.

items.prompt: For most SOLIDS like sugar, how does heating the solvent change how much dissolves?
choices: Heating lets more solid dissolve
Heating lets less solid dissolve
Temperature makes no difference at all
Heating turns the solid into a gas
items.explanation: For most solids, warming the solvent raises solubility, so more dissolves — sugar dissolves more readily in hot tea than in iced tea. Less-dissolving (choice 2) describes gases, not solids; temperature clearly matters (choice 3); and the solid dissolves, it does not become a gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why does a warm fizzy drink go flat faster than a cold one?
choices: Because warm water dissolves more gas
Because the gas turns into sugar when warm
Because a gas is LESS soluble when warm, so it escapes more easily
Because cold drinks have no gas in them
items.explanation: Gases behave opposite to most solids: they are less soluble when warm, so the dissolved gas leaves the drink faster and it goes flat. Warm liquid holds less gas, not more (choice 1); the gas does not become sugar (choice 2); and cold drinks do contain dissolved gas (choice 4).

items.prompt: Greasy dishes are hard to clean with water alone, but soap helps. This best illustrates that:
choices: Water easily dissolves nonpolar grease on its own
Grease is nonpolar, so polar water cannot dissolve it well — soap acts as a go-between
Soap makes water nonpolar so it stops working
Grease and water are the same kind of substance
items.explanation: Grease is nonpolar and water is polar, so 'like dissolves like' says water alone struggles — soap bridges the two so the grease can be carried away. Water does not easily dissolve grease by itself (choice 1), soap helps rather than disabling water (choice 3), and grease and water are very different types (choice 4).

lessons.title: Concentration, Separating Mixtures, and Cloudy Ones
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Weak coffee and strong coffee are made of the same two things — coffee and water — yet one is gentle and the other packs a punch. The only difference is **how much** coffee is in the water. That idea has a name: concentration.

**Build.** **Concentration** is **how much solute is in a given amount of solution**. A **dilute** solution has only a **little** solute — it is weak. A **concentrated** solution has a **lot** of solute — it is strong. Weak versus strong coffee or juice is exactly this. To make a strong drink weaker you **dilute** it: **add more solvent** (usually water) to spread the same solute through more liquid. Note a common trap: 'concentrated' is about the **amount of solute per amount of solution**, not about having a large volume — a tiny cup of espresso is very concentrated even though there is not much of it.
